---
title: "BS: What Did Early Humans Think About the Beach?"
description: "An entirely unrigorous history of why humans have always been drawn to the shoreline — from shell middens to sunscreen."
author: "Michael Friendly"
date: "2026-06-16"
draft: true
categories: [beach science, anthropology, humor]
---

I should be transparent: this is not a subject I knew anything about before
this afternoon. The details below come from off-beach research — conducted,
in the finest BS tradition, at a beach bar with wifi, a cold drink, and the
Mediterranean glittering approximately twelve metres away. I accept that this
may not meet the methodological standards of a conventional literature review.

I maintain that it meets higher ones.

---

## The Beach as Pantry

The earliest evidence of humans at the coast is not spiritual — it is hungry.

Shell middens, the ancient refuse heaps of people who ate a great many
shellfish, are among the oldest traces of systematic human food gathering
anywhere on earth. Sites along the South African coast at Pinnacle Point and
Blombos Cave date back 160,000–170,000 years, placing our ancestors on the
beach during a period when much of the African interior was becoming drier and
less hospitable. The coast, by contrast, was reliable. Shellfish do not run
away. Tidal pools are predictable. The sea does not have a bad season.

The beach was, in short, a supermarket. Open daily, well-stocked, no
membership required.

---

## The Beach That Made Us Smarter

Here is where it gets genuinely interesting. Some researchers have argued that
coastal living did not merely feed early humans — it may have accelerated
their cognitive development.

The argument runs as follows: shellfish, fish, and other marine foods are
exceptionally rich in the omega-3 fatty acids — particularly DHA — that are
critical for brain development and function. A population eating reliably from
the sea would have had a sustained nutritional advantage over inland
populations subsisting on leaner diets. Over tens of thousands of years, that
advantage compounds.

On this view, the beach was not just a refuge during a difficult climate
period. It was a cognitive incubator. The first Beach Scientists may have been
beach scientists in a more literal sense than intended — the diet sharpened the
mind that asked the questions.

This remains a hypothesis rather than settled science, and it has its critics.
But it is a wonderful hypothesis, and one that is best contemplated at a beach
bar.

---

## The Beach as Threshold

Move forward in time — still prehistoric, but into the era of ritual and
symbol — and the beach takes on a different character entirely.

The boundary between land and sea is what anthropologists call a *liminal
space*: a threshold between the known and the unknown, the controllable and
the uncontrollable. Nearly every ancient culture that lived near the coast
treated the shoreline as sacred ground in some form. The sea was generous and
murderous in equal measure. It gave fish and took fishermen. It connected
distant peoples and drowned those who miscalculated it. No wonder people left
offerings at its edge.

Shell ornaments found at Blombos Cave — perforated and apparently worn as
jewellery — date to around 75,000 years ago, making them among the earliest
evidence of symbolic thinking in the human record. They were made on the
coast, from materials the coast provided. Whether they carried spiritual
meaning we cannot say with certainty. But the impulse to make something
beautiful from what the beach offered is recognisably human, and very old.

---

## Burials, Rituals, and the Water's Edge

Many ancient and indigenous cultures chose the water's edge for ceremonies
marking life's transitions — birth, death, initiation, the turning of seasons.
The logic is intuitive even now: the sea is vast and indifferent and permanent
in a way that makes human concerns feel appropriately small. Standing at the
shoreline, you are at the edge of something that does not care about you, which
is either terrifying or clarifying depending on your disposition.

The Greeks had Poseidon; the Celts believed the sea connected the living world
to the otherworld; Pacific island cultures developed elaborate cosmologies
centred on the ocean that shaped their navigation, their agriculture, and their
understanding of time. In each case the sea was not background — it was an
active presence in the moral and spiritual universe.

---

## And Here We Are

160,000 years after the first humans crouched over tidal pools at Pinnacle
Point, we are still here. Still drawn to the edge of the water. Still finding
something at the shoreline that we do not quite find elsewhere — a combination
of abundance and humility, stimulation and calm, the familiar and the
genuinely unknown.

The shell middens have been replaced by beach bars. The offerings to sea gods
have been replaced by sunscreen and Kindles. But the pull is the same, and it
is very old.

Beach Science, it turns out, did not begin this summer at Leucate Plage. It
began on a South African coast 160,000 years ago, with someone picking up a
shellfish and wondering — however wordlessly — what else might be out there.

---

*Next time you find yourself staring at the horizon, you are participating in
one of the oldest human behaviours on record. Act accordingly.*
